Job rebukes his friends for thinking they were wiser than he was. We are quick to consider corrections as insults, and to feel mocked when we receive advice; this is our mistake; but here was reason for his accusation. He believed the real cause of their behavior was that they looked down on him because he had fallen into poverty. This is how the world works. Even an honest person, if he faces hardship, is regarded with contempt.

Job appeals to facts. The most daring thieves, oppressors, and ungodly people often prosper. However, this does not happen by chance; the Lord arranges these things. Worldly success is of little value in his sight: he has better things for his children. Job explains everything as stemming from God’s complete ownership of all creation. He asks his friends to allow him to judge what they have said; he appeals to any fair judgment.

This is a fine discussion by Job concerning the wisdom, power, and sovereignty of God in managing all the affairs of people according to the counsel of His own will, which no one can resist. It would be beneficial if wise and good people who disagree about minor things would see how it honors them and comforts them, as well as benefits others, to focus on the major points where they agree. There are no complaints or reflections here. He gives many examples of God’s powerful management of people, overruling all their plans and overcoming all their opposition. Having all strength and wisdom, God knows how to use even those who are foolish and wicked; otherwise there is so little wisdom and honesty in the world that it would have been in chaos and ruin long ago. These important truths were meant to convince the disputants that they were mistaken in trying to explain why God afflicted Job; his ways are unsearchable, and his judgments cannot be understood. Let us observe what beautiful illustrations there are in the word of God confirming his sovereignty and wisdom in that sovereignty: but the highest and most important is that the Lord Jesus was crucified by the malice of the Jews; and who but the Lord could have known that this one event would save the world?
